digitraffic 0.0.0.9006

Getting started with digitraffic

Source: vignettes/digitraffic.Rmd
digitraffic.Rmd

What is Digitraffic?

Digitraffic is an open API maintained by Finntraffic that provides real-time and historical traffic data from Finland’s road network. The data comes from over 450 LAM (Liikenteen Automaattinen Mittaus / Automatic Traffic Measurement) stations — electromagnetic induction loops embedded in the road surface that detect every passing vehicle.

Each LAM station records:

  • Speed (km/h)
  • Vehicle length (metres) — used for classification
  • Direction and lane
  • Headway — time gap to the previous vehicle
  • Vehicle classification — 7 categories from cars to HCT trucks

The digitraffic R package wraps these API endpoints and returns all data as tidy tibbles, ready for analysis with dplyr and visualisation with ggplot2.

Installation 

# install.packages("pak")
pak::pak("bbtheo/digitraffic")

Loading the package 

library(digitraffic)
library(dplyr)

Discovering stations

The first thing you’ll want to do is find stations of interest. dt_stations() returns a tibble of all LAM stations with their coordinates, bearing, and operational status:

stations <- dt_stations()
stations

The result is cached for 5 minutes so repeated calls are instant. dt_stations() supports five filter parameters — see vignette("station-filtering") for the full guide. Quick examples:

# By name (case-insensitive regex)
dt_stations(name = "Espoo")

# By road number (parsed from name, no extra API calls)
dt_stations(road_number = 1)

# By geographic bounding box
dt_stations(bbox = c(24.5, 60.1, 25.2, 60.4))

# By municipality or province (uses bundled metadata cache)
dt_stations(municipality = "Espoo")
dt_stations(province = "Uusimaa")

# Filters combine — road 4 stations in Pirkanmaa
dt_stations(road_number = 4, province = "Pirkanmaa")

Detailed station metadata

For a single station, dt_station() returns an extended set of 32 fields including municipality, province, road number, road section, free-flow speeds, and multilingual names:

detail <- dt_station(23001)
detail

# Key fields
detail |>
  select(name, municipality, province, road_number,
         free_flow_speed1, free_flow_speed2)

Mapping stations with sf

If you have the sf package installed, you can convert the stations tibble to a spatial object for mapping:

library(sf)

stations_sf <- dt_stations() |>
  st_as_sf(coords = c("longitude", "latitude"), crs = 4326)

# Filter to active stations only
active <- stations_sf |>
  filter(collection_status == "GATHERING")

plot(active["bearing"], pch = 20, main = "LAM stations coloured by bearing")

Real-time sensor data

dt_station_data() fetches the current sensor readings for one station. Data is updated approximately every minute:

data <- dt_station_data(23001)
data

Each row is a single sensor measurement. The name column contains a structured Finnish name encoding the measurement type, interval, and direction. For example:

Sensor name Meaning
OHITUKSET_5MIN_KIINTEA_SUUNTA1 Vehicle count, 5-min fixed interval, direction 1
KESKINOPEUS_60MIN_KIINTEA_SUUNTA1 Average speed, 60-min fixed interval, direction 1
OHITUKSET_5MIN_LIUKUVA_SUUNTA2_KAISTA1 Vehicle count, 5-min sliding, direction 2, lane 1

Filtering to speed readings 

data |>
  filter(unit == "km/h") |>
  select(name, value, unit, time_window_start, time_window_end)

Filtering to volume readings 

data |>
  filter(unit == "kpl/h") |>
  select(name, value, unit, time_window_start, time_window_end)

All stations at once

dt_stations_data() fetches sensor data for every station in one request. The response is several MB, so it takes a few seconds:

all_data <- dt_stations_data()

# How many stations have data?
n_distinct(all_data$station_id)

# Average 60-min speed across all stations, direction 1
all_data |>
  filter(name == "KESKINOPEUS_60MIN_KIINTEA_SUUNTA1") |>
  summarise(
    n_stations    = n(),
    mean_speed    = mean(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_speed  = median(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    min_speed     = min(value, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_speed     = max(value, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

Understanding sensor types

There are 76 different sensor types. dt_sensors() returns the full catalogue with descriptions:

sensors <- dt_sensors()
sensors

# Speed sensors
sensors |> filter(unit == "km/h")

# Volume sensors
sensors |> filter(unit == "kpl/h")

# Status / special sensors
sensors |> filter(unit == "***")

You can join this catalogue to sensor data to enrich the output:

dt_station_data(23001) |>
  left_join(
    sensors |> select(sensor_id = id, description_fi, direction),
    by = "sensor_id"
  )

Sensor constants (calibration values)

Each station has calibration constants that define reference values for that specific road segment:

# All constants
consts <- dt_sensor_constants()
consts

# Constants for one station
dt_sensor_constants(id = 23001)

The constants include:

Name Meaning
VVAPAAS1 Free-flow speed (km/h), direction 1
VVAPAAS2 Free-flow speed (km/h), direction 2
MS1 Maximum hourly capacity (vehicles/h), direction 1
MS2 Maximum hourly capacity (vehicles/h), direction 2
Tien_suunta Road bearing in degrees (0-360)

Many stations have seasonal variants with different summer (Apr-Oct) and winter (Nov-Mar) values:

dt_sensor_constants(id = 20002) |>
  filter(grepl("VVAPAAS", name)) |>
  select(name, value, valid_from, valid_to)
#> # A tibble: 4 x 4
#>   name     value valid_from valid_to
#>   <chr>    <dbl> <chr>      <chr>
#> 1 VVAPAAS1   105 04-01      10-31
#> 2 VVAPAAS1   100 11-01      03-31
#> 3 VVAPAAS2   105 04-01      10-31
#> 4 VVAPAAS2   100 11-01      03-31

Next steps

  • For filtering stations by road, municipality, province, and bounding box, see vignette("station-filtering")
  • For historical per-vehicle data, see vignette("historical-data")
  • For the full function reference, see the package documentation site
